source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
The diagonals of the squares $A B C D$ and $E F G B$ are $7 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ long respectively (see diagram). The point $P$ is the point of intersection of the two diagonals of the square $A B C D$. How big is the area of the triangle $F P D$ (in $\mathrm{cm}^{2}$)?
<image1>

image_dependency: The geometric arrangement of squares ABCD and EFGB, and the locations of F, P, and D, are not fully recoverable from text alone
multi_step: Solving requires combining properties of two different squares with given diagonal lengths
The statement 'P is the intersection of the two diagonals of square ABCD' adds an intermediate geometric inference about center/midpoint structure
Finding the area of triangle FPD likely needs deriving lengths or coordinates from the diagram before computing area
verifiability: A geometry-based solution can be checked using standard square-diagonal relationships and triangle area formulas

decision_reasons: Question, image, and numeric answer are all present, so the core semantics are recoverable.
Asset registry passed with no recorded issues, indicating good basic completeness.
High image_dependency_score is acceptable here because the required diagram asset exists.
Multi-step and verifiability scores are strong, making the sample valuable and checkable.
Although the diagram configuration is somewhat implicit, this is a manageable risk rather than a reason to defer or reject.
